_purpose: Local economy timeline for the Virginia Shenandoah Valley regional cluster (Frederick, Shenandoah, Page, Warren, Rockingham, Augusta, Rockbridge, Botetourt counties). Covers 1730-1900 across five economic eras — from frontier subsistence through postwar recovery. Designed to give Polsia the economic backdrop for any ancestor living in this eight-county region so that narrative context sentences reflect what people actually did for a living, what they earned, and why they stayed or left.
display_name: Virginia Shenandoah Valley — Local Economy Timeline

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Subsistence farming, livestock herding, fur trade, frontier land speculation
economic_eras.description: The earliest Valley settlers arrived via the Great Wagon Road from Pennsylvania — Scots-Irish, Germans, and English Quakers seeking cheap land beyond the Blue Ridge. The economy was initially subsistence: corn, rye, and hogs on small clearings, supplemented by hunting and the deerskin trade with remaining Native populations. Land was acquired through Lord Fairfax's Northern Neck grants (in the lower Valley) or through colonial patents from the governor's council (in the upper Valley). German settlers, particularly Mennonites and Brethren who arrived in the 1740s-1750s, brought superior agricultural techniques — deep plowing, systematic manuring, and crop rotation — that would transform the Valley from frontier to breadbasket within a generation. Wheat emerged as the primary cash crop by the 1760s, earlier than in most backcountry regions, because the limestone soils were naturally suited to it. The iron industry began in the mountain margins, with charcoal furnaces established in Rockbridge and Botetourt counties by the 1760s.

county_specifics.note: First settled county in the Valley. Winchester was a market town by the 1750s, serving both settlers and armies during the French and Indian War. Lord Fairfax's residency at Greenway Court drew surveyors and speculators, including the young George Washington.

Heavy German settlement. The North Fork valley was cleared for farming by the 1750s. Woodstock became the county seat when Dunmore County was formed in 1772. Stone and timber-frame farmsteads distinguished the German landscape from Scots-Irish log buildings.

The narrow Luray Valley between Massanutten and the Blue Ridge was settled later than the main Valley. Smaller farms and more subsistence-oriented economy. Part of Shenandoah and Rockingham counties in this era.
Part of Frederick County in this era. The confluence area at Front Royal was a ferry crossing and stopping point on the road north.

The broadest and most fertile section of the Valley floor. German Mennonite and Brethren families created intensive farms that produced surpluses earlier than most frontier areas. Part of Augusta County until 1778.

The 'Mother of Counties' — the administrative center for the entire western Virginia frontier. Staunton was the county seat and the primary market town for the upper Valley. Augusta's economy combined farming, livestock, and frontier services (surveying, legal work, Indian diplomacy).
More rugged terrain limited farming to the narrow valley floor. Iron ore deposits attracted the first furnace operators by the 1760s. Part of Augusta County until 1778.

Southern gateway where the Valley narrows. The Great Road funneled migrants through on the way to Kentucky and Tennessee. Botetourt's economy combined farming with iron production and the services of a corridor county — inns, blacksmiths, ferry operators.

key_infrastructure.name: Great Wagon Road (Philadelphia to the Valley)
key_infrastructure.impact: Primary migration and trade corridor. Brought settlers, merchants, and goods from Pennsylvania into the Valley. The road was the Valley's lifeline to the Atlantic economy.

key_infrastructure.name: Lord Fairfax's Northern Neck land office
key_infrastructure.impact: Controlled land distribution in the lower Valley (Frederick, Shenandoah, Warren). Grants at favorable terms attracted settlers from Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and Maryland.

key_infrastructure.name: First grist mills on Valley streams
key_infrastructure.impact: Mills converted wheat to flour, enabling market agriculture. By 1770, the Valley's mill concentration rivaled southeastern Pennsylvania.

major_employers.name: Individual farms (family and indentured labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of small operations. German farms were family-labor intensive; Scots-Irish farms relied more on indentured servants and later enslaved labor.

major_employers.name: Early charcoal iron furnaces
major_employers.employment_scale: Each furnace employed 20-50 workers, many enslaved.

wage_benchmarks.source: Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 2

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in the Valley in the {decade}s, the land was cheap and the soil was deep — the limestone ground grew wheat without the years of clearing that Tidewater tobacco demanded. A family could carve a farm from the open woodland in a season and have surplus grain to sell within two years.
economic_eras.source: Mitchell, Robert D. Commercialism and Frontier: Perspectives on the Early Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1977. Hofstra, Warren R. The Planting of New Virginia: Settlement and Landscape in the Shenandoah Valley. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2004.

economic_eras.era_name: The Wheat Kingdom — Valley Agriculture Matures
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Commercial wheat and flour production, livestock driving, charcoal iron industry, mixed farming
economic_eras.description: The Valley became Virginia's breadbasket during this era. While the Tidewater and Piedmont exhausted their soils growing tobacco, the limestone Valley floor — sustained by German agricultural practices and natural fertility — produced wheat in quantities that made the region one of the most productive agricultural districts in eastern North America. Flour milling became a major industry: water-powered merchant mills ground Valley wheat into flour for export via Winchester (north to Baltimore) and later via the James River corridor (east to Richmond). The Valley Turnpike, improved from a frontier trace to a macadamized road, became the commercial artery connecting farms to markets. Livestock driving was a parallel industry — cattle and hogs raised on Valley farms were driven on the hoof to eastern markets, particularly Philadelphia and Baltimore. The charcoal iron industry expanded in Rockbridge and Botetourt counties, consuming mountain timber and relying heavily on enslaved labor. By 1820, the Valley's economic structure was distinct from the rest of Virginia: wheat, not tobacco; diversified farming, not monoculture; free labor alongside enslaved, rather than overwhelmingly enslaved.

county_specifics.note: Winchester was the Valley's primary commercial center, with direct road connections to Baltimore and Alexandria. Flour mills on Opequon Creek processed wheat for export. The apple industry began in this era.
German farming practices produced exceptional yields. The stone bank barns of Shenandoah County stored grain quantities that rivaled Rockingham. New Market and Woodstock were local market centers.
The Luray Valley produced wheat on a smaller scale. Isolation east of Massanutten limited market access. Families combined farming with timbering on the mountain slopes.
The Front Royal area served as a transit point for Valley products moving north toward the Potomac and eastern markets.

The widest and most productive section of the Valley floor. Mennonite and Brethren farms achieved yields per acre comparable to southeastern Pennsylvania. Over 100 mill sites operated in the county by 1800. Harrisonburg grew as the central Valley's market town.
Staunton's position as regional capital was reinforced by its role as a market center. Three rivers provided abundant mill sites. Augusta County was one of Virginia's leading wheat producers.

Split economy: farming on the valley floor and iron production on the mountain margins. The iron furnaces (Lucy Selina, Bath, Buffalo Forge) employed large workforces, many enslaved. Lexington was a courthouse town and increasingly a college town (Washington College, 1749).
Iron furnaces at Eagle Rock and Longdale, plus the Great Road corridor economy. Buchanan on the James River was a transportation terminus. Fincastle remained a small courthouse town.

key_infrastructure.name: Valley Turnpike improvements
key_infrastructure.impact: Macadamization of the main Valley road created a reliable all-weather surface from Winchester to Staunton. Toll gates charged per wagon, but the road cut travel time and wagon-wreck losses dramatically.

key_infrastructure.name: Merchant flour mills proliferation
key_infrastructure.impact: Over 200 merchant mills operated across the cluster by 1830. Mills were the Valley's primary value-added industry, converting 60-cent bushels of wheat into $4-$6 barrels of flour.

key_infrastructure.name: Livestock driving routes to eastern markets
key_infrastructure.impact: Cattle and hog drives from the Valley to Baltimore, Philadelphia, and Richmond created seasonal employment for drovers and enriched tavern owners along the routes.

major_employers.name: Wheat farms and flour mills
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment mode across the cluster. Milling employed skilled laborers at wages above farm rates.

major_employers.name: Charcoal iron furnaces
major_employers.employment_scale: Each furnace employed 30-100 workers. Enslaved ironworkers were among the most valuable enslaved people in Virginia. Charles Dew estimates Buffalo Forge alone employed over 60 enslaved workers at its peak.

major_employers.name: Valley Turnpike maintenance and toll operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Dozens of toll-keepers, road workers, and associated tavern operators.

wage_benchmarks.source: Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 3
Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 5

economic_eras.narrative_hook: By the time {ancestor_name} was farming in {county} County in the {decade}s, the Valley was Virginia's breadbasket — the wheat grew thick on limestone soil that did not exhaust the way Tidewater tobacco land did, and every creek junction had a mill where {pronoun_subject} could convert a wagon-load of grain into flour worth three times the wheat.
economic_eras.source: Mitchell, Robert D. Commercialism and Frontier. Charlottesville: UPVa, 1977. Koons, Kenneth E., and Warren R. Hofstra, eds. After the Backcountry: Rural Life in the Great Valley of Virginia, 1800-1900. Knoxville: UT Press, 2000. Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: Norton, 1994.

economic_eras.era_name: Railroad and Canal Era — Antebellum Peak
economic_eras.dominant_economy: Commercial agriculture (wheat, livestock, flour) enhanced by railroad and canal connections; iron industry at peak; institutional growth (VMI, colleges, asylums)
economic_eras.description: The railroad and canal transformed the Valley economy in the 1840s and 1850s. The Virginia Central Railroad reached Staunton in 1854, connecting the upper Valley to Charlottesville and Richmond for the first time by rail. The Winchester & Potomac Railroad (1836) connected Winchester to the B&O at Harpers Ferry, opening the northern Valley to Baltimore markets. The James River and Kanawha Canal reached Buchanan in Botetourt County by 1857, creating a water route from the southern Valley to Richmond. These transportation links turned the Valley from a regional agricultural district into a major grain-exporting region. Wheat prices rose as shipping costs fell. The iron industry reached its antebellum peak, with furnaces in Rockbridge and Botetourt shipping pig iron by canal and later by rail. Institutional growth — VMI (1839), the Virginia School for the Deaf and Blind in Staunton (1839), Augusta Female Seminary (later Mary Baldwin), the Western Lunatic Asylum — made the upper Valley a center of education and social services. Enslaved labor was pervasive: the 1860 census recorded over 20,000 enslaved people across the eight counties, concentrated on the larger farms and in the iron industry.

county_specifics.note: The Winchester & Potomac Railroad (1836) connected Winchester to the B&O mainline at Harpers Ferry, reorienting the lower Valley's trade from Philadelphia to Baltimore. Winchester's economy boomed with flour milling, livestock trading, and mercantile activity.

The Manassas Gap Railroad reached Strasburg by 1854, providing the first rail connection. New Market thrived as a secondary market town. The Valley Turnpike remained essential for farms between rail stops.
No railroad penetrated the Luray Valley before the war. Page County remained the most isolated county in the cluster, dependent on wagon roads over Massanutten or through the Blue Ridge gaps.
The Manassas Gap Railroad terminus at Front Royal (by 1854) connected Warren County to eastern Virginia. Front Royal became a rail-and-river transit point.

No railroad reached Harrisonburg before the war. The Valley Turnpike was Rockingham's primary link to markets. Despite this, the county's agricultural productivity was among the highest in Virginia — the farms produced so much wheat that the turnpike wagons were a continuous stream at harvest time.

The Virginia Central Railroad reached Staunton in 1854 — the most transformative economic event in the county's antebellum history. Staunton became the western railroad terminus and a major shipping and distribution center. Institutions multiplied.
The James River canal reached Lexington by 1860, connecting the county to Richmond by water. VMI (1839) and Washington College anchored the institutional economy. Iron furnaces shipped by canal.

The James River and Kanawha Canal reached Buchanan by 1857 — the canal's western terminus. Iron, wheat, and timber loaded onto canal boats for the journey to Lynchburg and Richmond. The canal transformed Buchanan from a crossroads into a commercial basin.

key_infrastructure.name: Winchester & Potomac Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected Winchester to the B&O at Harpers Ferry. First railroad in the Valley. Reoriented northern Valley trade toward Baltimore.

key_infrastructure.name: Virginia Central Railroad to Staunton
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected the upper Valley to Richmond. Most important single piece of infrastructure in the cluster — made Staunton a regional capital.

key_infrastructure.name: James River and Kanawha Canal to Buchanan
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected the southern Valley (Rockbridge, Botetourt) to Richmond by water. The canal's Buchanan terminus was the western end of Virginia's internal improvement system.

key_infrastructure.name: Manassas Gap Railroad to Strasburg/Front Royal
key_infrastructure.impact: Connected the middle Valley (Shenandoah, Warren) to eastern Virginia via Manassas Junction.

major_employers.name: Commercial farms and flour mills
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment mode. Railroad access increased the value of Valley wheat by 20-30% by reducing shipping costs.

major_employers.name: Iron furnaces (Rockbridge, Botetourt)
major_employers.employment_scale: 10-12 active furnaces across the two counties. Peak employment: several hundred workers, majority enslaved.

major_employers.name: Railroad construction and operation
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of construction workers (many Irish immigrants and enslaved laborers); permanent railroad employees in dozens.

major_employers.name: VMI, colleges, asylums (institutional employment)
major_employers.employment_scale: Faculty, staff, and service workers — including enslaved people hired to institutions.

wage_benchmarks.source: Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 4
Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 6
Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 4

economic_eras.narrative_hook: When the railroad reached {county} County in {year}, {ancestor_name}'s wheat suddenly competed on a world stage — flour that had taken a week by wagon to reach Richmond now arrived in a day, and the price per bushel went up because the shipping cost went down.
economic_eras.source: Koons, Kenneth E., and Warren R. Hofstra, eds. After the Backcountry. Knoxville: UT Press, 2000. Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron. New York: Norton, 1994. Dunaway, Wayland Fuller. History of the James River and Kanawha Company. New York: Columbia University Press, 1922.

economic_eras.era_name: Civil War — The Valley Campaigns and Sheridan's Burning
economic_eras.dominant_economy: War economy: requisitioning, destruction, and military employment
economic_eras.description: The Shenandoah Valley was the Confederacy's breadbasket and its most fought-over territory. Jackson's 1862 Valley Campaign, the 1864 campaigns of Hunter and Sheridan, and constant skirmishing transformed the Valley from Virginia's richest agricultural district into a wasteland. Sheridan's October 1864 'Burning' was the decisive economic event: Grant ordered Sheridan to destroy the Valley's agricultural capacity so thoroughly that 'crows flying over it for the balance of this season will have to carry their provender with them.' Sheridan's forces burned over 2,000 barns, 70 mills, and drove off or slaughtered tens of thousands of livestock across the eight counties. The destruction was worst in Rockingham and Augusta — the richest counties with the most to burn. The iron furnaces in Rockbridge and Botetourt were wrecked. The railroad was torn up. The James River canal was damaged. The agricultural economy that had taken a century to build was destroyed in weeks.

county_specifics.note: Winchester changed hands an estimated 72 times. The town was garrisoned, fought over, and exhausted. Three major battles (1862, 1863, 1864) were fought near Winchester. Sheridan's Burning destroyed the surrounding farms.
The Battle of Fisher's Hill (September 22, 1864) and the Battle of Cedar Creek (October 19, 1864) were fought in Shenandoah County. Sheridan's forces burned farms systematically after Cedar Creek.
Jackson used the Luray Valley as a flanking corridor before the Battle of Front Royal. Page County saw less systematic destruction than the main Valley but suffered from foraging and skirmishing.
The Battle of Front Royal (May 23, 1862) put Warren County on the military map. The town changed hands repeatedly.

The richest county hit hardest. Sheridan's forces burned more barns and mills in Rockingham than in any other county. The Mennonite and Brethren communities, who had opposed the war on religious grounds, watched their life's work destroyed.

Hit twice: Hunter's raid damaged Staunton in June 1864, and Sheridan's forces completed the destruction in October. The railroad was torn up. The institutional infrastructure (schools, asylum) was disrupted.
Hunter burned VMI and parts of Lexington in June 1864. The iron furnaces were destroyed or disabled. Washington College survived but was damaged.
Hunter's force passed through en route to Lynchburg. The iron furnaces at Eagle Rock and Longdale were damaged. The canal was disrupted.

key_infrastructure.name: Systematic destruction of agricultural infrastructure
key_infrastructure.impact: Over 2,000 barns, 70 mills, and tens of thousands of livestock destroyed across the cluster. The Valley's agricultural capacity was eliminated for at least two growing seasons.

key_infrastructure.name: Railroad destruction (Virginia Central, W&P, Manassas Gap)
key_infrastructure.impact: All Valley railroads were wrecked by one side or the other. Bridges, depots, and rolling stock destroyed. Rebuilding took until the early 1870s.

key_infrastructure.name: James River canal damaged
key_infrastructure.impact: The canal, which had just reached Buchanan, was damaged and never fully recovered. The postwar C&O Railroad would replace it.

major_employers.name: Confederate Army (conscription and volunteering)
major_employers.employment_scale: Thousands of men from the cluster served. The Stonewall Brigade, recruited largely from the Valley counties, was among the most famous Confederate units.

major_employers.name: Quartermaster and commissary operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Both armies requisitioned food, forage, horses, and labor from Valley farms. This was not 'employment' but extraction.

economic_eras.narrative_hook: The war reached {ancestor_name}'s {county} County in {year} and took everything the land had built — the barn that had stored a century's worth of harvests, the mill that had ground the wheat, the fences that had kept the cattle in, and the men who had done the work.
economic_eras.source: Heatwole, John L. The Burning: Sheridan in the Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998. Robertson, James I., Jr. Stonewall Jackson: The Man, the Soldier, the Legend. New York: Macmillan, 1997.

economic_eras.dominant_economy: Agricultural recovery, railroad-based market agriculture, extractive industries (timber, minerals), orchard industry emergence
economic_eras.description: Recovery from the Burning was the defining economic experience of the postwar Valley. The Mennonite and Brethren communities of Rockingham County rebuilt fastest, pooling community labor through barn raisings and cooperative marketing. The railroads — rebuilt and expanded as the Chesapeake & Ohio (replacing the Virginia Central and James River canal) and the B&O (through the northern Valley) — reconnected the Valley to national markets by the early 1870s. Agriculture diversified: wheat remained important but was joined by livestock, orchard fruits (particularly apples in Frederick County), and dairy. The charcoal iron industry declined as coke-fired furnaces in Pennsylvania and Alabama undercut Valley iron prices. Timber extraction expanded in the mountain margins as railroad logging penetrated previously inaccessible forests. Former enslaved people in the Valley faced the same constraints as elsewhere in the South — limited land access, sharecropping, and wage labor — but the Valley's smaller-scale slaveholding meant the transition was less dramatic than in the plantation South. Robert E. Lee's presidency of Washington College (1865-1870) and Stonewall Jackson's burial at Lexington made the upper Valley a center of Lost Cause memory.

county_specifics.note: The apple orchard industry began its rise, benefiting from the B&O Railroad's connection to urban markets. Winchester evolved from a war-ravaged garrison town to a commercial center focused on agriculture and fruit processing.
German farming communities rebuilt steadily. The iron furnaces at Shenandoah Iron Works declined. Tourism began as a minor industry — the natural scenery attracted visitors from eastern cities.
Remained the most isolated county. The Luray Caverns discovery (1878) and subsequent tourism began to supplement the agricultural economy.
Front Royal recovered slowly. The railroad through town supported modest commercial activity. Tourism to the mountain gaps and springs emerged.

Rebuilt fastest thanks to Mennonite and Brethren community networks. Poultry production emerged as a new industry by the 1890s. The cooperative marketing traditions of the peace-church communities laid the foundation for modern agribusiness.

Staunton's railroad connections (C&O) were restored by the early 1870s, and the town resumed its role as regional capital. Institutional employment (schools, asylum) recovered. Augusta diversified into livestock and dairy.

The iron industry declined sharply. Lee's presidency of Washington College (renamed Washington and Lee after his death in 1870) and VMI's rebuilding made institutional employment increasingly important. Goshen Pass attracted tourists.
The C&O Railroad through the James River gap replaced the canal and brought new extractive industries — timber and minerals. Eagle Rock and the mountain communities shifted from iron to lumber.

key_infrastructure.name: C&O Railroad (replacing Virginia Central and James River canal)
key_infrastructure.impact: The C&O connected the Valley through the Allegheny Mountains to the Ohio River, creating a transcontinental freight corridor. Staunton and the James River gap became major shipping points.

key_infrastructure.name: B&O Railroad expansion in the northern Valley
key_infrastructure.impact: B&O connections through Winchester and the northern Valley maintained the lower Valley's orientation toward Baltimore and the north.

key_infrastructure.name: Shenandoah Valley Railroad
key_infrastructure.impact: Branch line through the Valley from Hagerstown to Lexington, filling gaps between the main corridors and connecting counties that had lacked direct rail service.

key_infrastructure.name: Apple cold storage and processing facilities
key_infrastructure.impact: Frederick County's apple industry required cold storage and processing infrastructure. Winchester became the apple capital of Virginia.

major_employers.name: Rebuilt farms (family, hired, and sharecropping labor)
major_employers.employment_scale: Dominant employment mode. Free Black workers entered the agricultural labor force as wage hands and sharecroppers.

major_employers.name: C&O and B&O Railroad operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Hundreds of permanent employees; section gangs, depot workers, and train crews.

major_employers.name: Timber and lumber operations
major_employers.employment_scale: Seasonal and itinerant workforce. Railroad logging opened mountain forests that had been inaccessible before.

major_employers.name: Washington and Lee University, VMI
major_employers.employment_scale: Faculty, staff, and service workers. Lee's presidency attracted national attention and enrollment.

wage_benchmarks.source: Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 8
Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 9
Koons and Hofstra, After the Backcountry, ch. 9
Wayland, History of Rockingham County, Appendix

economic_eras.narrative_hook: After the Burning, {ancestor_name}'s {county} County rebuilt from the ground up — new barns raised where the old ones had burned, the railroad tracks relaid, and the wheat fields replanted. The Valley that had been Virginia's breadbasket before the war slowly became it again, though the world it fed had changed.
economic_eras.source: Koons, Kenneth E., and Warren R. Hofstra, eds. After the Backcountry. Knoxville: UT Press, 2000. Heatwole, The Burning. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998.

economic_turning_points.impact: First railroad in the Valley, connecting Winchester to the B&O at Harpers Ferry and reorienting the northern Valley's trade toward Baltimore.
Connected the upper Valley to Richmond. The single most important economic turning point in the cluster — Staunton became a rail-connected regional capital.

economic_turning_points.event: James River and Kanawha Canal reaches Buchanan
economic_turning_points.impact: Connected the southern Valley (Botetourt, Rockbridge) to Richmond by water. The canal's maximum western extent.
Systematic destruction of the Valley's agricultural infrastructure — barns, mills, livestock. The most devastating single economic event in the cluster's history.

economic_turning_points.event: C&O Railroad completed through the Alleghenies
economic_turning_points.impact: Connected the Valley to the Ohio River valley and national markets. Replaced the James River canal and transformed the economic geography of the southern Valley.

narrative_hooks: The Valley economy that {ancestor_name} knew was built on two foundations: limestone soil that grew wheat without exhausting, and water that powered mills at every creek junction — and the combination made the Shenandoah Valley the breadbasket of Virginia while the Tidewater was wearing out its tobacco land.

When the railroad reached {county} County, it transformed {ancestor_name}'s economic world — wheat that had taken a week to reach market by wagon could now arrive in a day, and the price difference between roadside and railside land told the story.

The charcoal iron furnaces on the mountains above {ancestor_name}'s Valley farm consumed timber at a rate of 200-300 acres per year — and the enslaved workers who fed those furnaces lived in a world as industrial as Pittsburgh while the valley floor below remained pastoral.

Sheridan's Burning destroyed the Valley that {ancestor_name}'s family had spent a century building — but the soil remained, and the springs still ran, and the rebuilding began before the smoke cleared.

The Mennonite and Brethren communities of the central Valley rebuilt faster than anyone because they had something the rest of the South lacked — a tradition of communal labor, barn raisings, and mutual aid that turned individual loss into collective recovery.
source_references: Mitchell, Robert D. Commercialism and Frontier: Perspectives on the Early Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1977.
Hofstra, Warren R. The Planting of New Virginia: Settlement and Landscape in the Shenandoah Valley. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2004.
Koons, Kenneth E., and Warren R. Hofstra, eds. After the Backcountry: Rural Life in the Great Valley of Virginia, 1800-1900. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 2000.
Dew, Charles B. Bond of Iron: Master and Slave at Buffalo Forge. New York: W.W. Norton, 1994.
Heatwole, John L. The Burning: Sheridan in the Shenandoah Valley. Charlottesville: Rockbridge Publishing, 1998.
Dunaway, Wayland Fuller. History of the James River and Kanawha Company. New York: Columbia University Press, 1922.
Wust, Klaus. The Virginia Germans. Charlottesville: University Press of Virginia, 1969.
Wright, Gavin. Old South, New South: Revolutions in the Southern Economy since the Civil War. New York: Basic Books, 1986.
